title: Find nth node
description: Find the nth node from the beginning (1-based index)

examples.explanation:

Step 2: Check: current not null (node(1)) and count=1 < n=3 → true
Step 3: Move current = current.next = node(2), increment count=2
Step 4: Check: current not null (node(2)) and count=2 < n=3 → true
Step 5: Move current = current.next = node(3), increment count=3
Step 6: Check: current not null (node(3)) and count=3 < n=3 → false (count not < n)
Step 7: Loop ends, current=node(3) not null → return node(3)

Step 1: Initialize current = node(10), count = 1
Step 2: Check: current not null (node(10)) and count=1 < n=5 → true
Step 3: Move current = current.next = node(20), increment count=2
Step 4: Check: current not null (node(20)) and count=2 < n=5 → true
Step 5: Move current = current.next = null, increment count=3
Step 6: Check: current=null → false (current is null)
Step 7: Loop ends, current=null → return null

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the linked list from the head, counting nodes until reaching the nth node (1-based index). This is straightforward and works by directly accessing each node in sequence.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find nth node from beginning in linked list using brute force
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the nth node (1-based index) from the beginning of a linked list by iterating through nodes.
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach to finding the nth node from the beginning of a linked list is straightforward and intuitive. Since linked lists don't support random access like arrays, we must traverse the list sequentially from the head node. The intuition is simple: start at the head, move forward one node at a time while counting, and stop when we've reached the nth position. This works because each node contains a reference to the next node, creating a chain we can follow. The algorithm begins by initializing a current pointer to the head and a counter to 1. We then enter a loop that continues while the current node exists and our counter hasn't reached n. In each iteration, we move current to current.next and increment the counter. When the loop ends, if current is not null, we've found the nth node; otherwise, n exceeds the list length. The time complexity is O(n) in the worst case where we might traverse the entire list, and space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few pointers. This connects directly to the problem statement by providing a complete working solution. Common mistakes include using 0-based indexing when the problem specifies 1-based, not handling cases where n exceeds list length, and off-by-one errors in the loop condition. The real code pattern involves simple pointer manipulation and counting.

Pseudocode:
function findNthNode(head, n):
    current = head
    count = 1
    while current != null and count < n:
        current = current.next
        count = count + 1
    if current == null:
        return null  # or appropriate error
    return current

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize current = node(1), count = 1
Step 2: Check: current not null (node(1)) and count=1 < n=3 → true
Step 3: Move current = current.next = node(2), increment count=2
Step 4: Check: current not null (node(2)) and count=2 < n=3 → true
Step 5: Move current = current.next = node(3), increment count=3
Step 6: Check: current not null (node(3)) and count=3 < n=3 → false (count not < n)
Step 7: Loop ends, current=node(3) not null → return node(3)

Step 1: Initialize current = node(10), count = 1
Step 2: Check: current not null (node(10)) and count=1 < n=5 → true
Step 3: Move current = current.next = node(20), increment count=2
Step 4: Check: current not null (node(20)) and count=2 < n=5 → true
Step 5: Move current = current.next = null, increment count=3
Step 6: Check: current=null → false (current is null)
Step 7: Loop ends, current=null → return null

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traverse linked list to a specific position
ladders.desc: The core traversal logic that moves through the linked list until reaching a target position.
ladders.explanation:
The key sub-routine in finding the nth node is the traversal mechanism that moves a pointer through the linked list to a specific position. This is the heart of the brute force approach. The intuition is that we need to follow the chain of next pointers from the head, advancing one node at a time, until we've moved the desired number of steps. This works because each node's next pointer provides the only way to access subsequent nodes in a singly linked list. The algorithm takes a starting node (typically the head) and a target position n. We initialize a current pointer to the start and a counter to 1 (for 1-based indexing). Then we enter a loop: while current is not null and counter is less than n, we set current = current.next and increment counter. The loop stops when either we reach the end of the list (current becomes null) or we've taken n-1 steps (counter reaches n). After the loop, current points to the nth node if it exists, or null if n exceeds the list length. The time complexity is O(n) as we may traverse up to n nodes, and space is O(1). This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by providing the positioning logic; the full problem just adds input validation and output handling. Common mistakes include starting counter at 0 instead of 1 for 1-based indexing, using while(current && counter <= n) which would overshoot by one, and not checking for null before accessing current.next. The real code pattern is a simple while loop with pointer advancement.

Pseudocode:
function traverseToPosition(start, n):
    current = start
    count = 1
    while current != null and count < n:
        current = current.next
        count = count + 1
    return current

Step 6: Check: current not null and count=3 < 3 → false
Step 7: Return current=node(3)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sequential node access with counter
ladders.desc: The fundamental technique of accessing nodes one by one while counting steps.
ladders.explanation:
The core logic of the brute force approach is the combination of sequential node access and step counting. This is the central insight that makes the solution work. The intuition is that in a linked list, we can only access nodes in order from first to last, so to reach the nth node, we must start at the beginning and move forward n-1 times. This works because each node contains a reference to the next node, creating a linear sequence. The algorithm doesn't require any complex data structures—just a pointer to track the current node and an integer to count how many nodes we've visited. We initialize the current pointer to the head node and set count to 1 (representing the first node). Then we repeatedly: check if we need to move further (count < n and current is not null), if so, advance current to the next node and increment count. The key insight is that after k iterations, current points to the (k+1)th node and count equals k+1. When count equals n, current points to the nth node. The time complexity is proportional to n (O(n)), and space is constant (O(1)). This logic feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the counting mechanism; the sub-routine packages this with proper loop control. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in the loop condition (e.g., using count <= n would move past the nth node), forgetting to check for null before advancing, and misunderstanding 1-based vs 0-based indexing. The real code pattern is a while loop with a condition that checks both position and null.

Pseudocode:
current = startNode
count = 1
while current != null and count < targetPosition:
    current = current.next
    count = count + 1
# After loop, current is at targetPosition or null

Step 2: Check: A not null and 1<3 → true, move current=B, count=2
Step 3: Check: B not null and 2<3 → true, move current=C, count=3

Result: current points to C, count=3

ladders.title: Building Block — Linked list traversal with a pointer
ladders.desc: The fundamental skill of moving through a linked list using node references.
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on the essential operation of traversing a linked list using a pointer. This is an intermediate concept needed before implementing the core logic. The intuition is that to access nodes in a linked list, we must follow the chain of next pointers from one node to the next. Unlike arrays where we can jump directly to any index, linked lists require sequential access. This works because each node contains data and a reference (pointer) to the next node. The algorithm starts with a pointer (often called 'current') pointing to the head node. To move to the next node, we update current to current.next. We continue this process until current becomes null, indicating we've reached the end of the list. The key insight is that current.next gives us access to the subsequent node, allowing us to traverse the entire list. The time complexity for traversing k nodes is O(k), and space is O(1) since we only use one extra pointer. This feeds into the core logic by providing the mechanism for sequential access; the core logic adds counting to this traversal. Common mistakes include not checking if current is null before accessing current.next (which causes null pointer errors), losing the head pointer by modifying it directly, and infinite loops if the list has cycles. The real code pattern is a simple loop that advances a pointer.

Pseudocode:
function traverseList(head):
    current = head
    while current != null:
        # Process current node here
        current = current.next
# This visits each node once

examples.trace:
Step 1: current = node(1)
Step 2: current not null → process node(1), current = node(2)
Step 3: current not null → process node(2), current = node(3)
Step 4: current not null → process node(3), current = null
Step 5: current null → stop

Step 1: current = node(10)
Step 2: current not null → process node(10), current = node(20)
Step 3: current not null → process node(20), current = null
Step 4: current null → stop

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing next node via pointer
ladders.desc: The fundamental operation of moving from one linked list node to the next using the next reference.
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation is the atomic action of advancing from one node to the next in a linked list. It's a fundamental operation used throughout linked list manipulation. The intuition is simple: each node in a singly linked list contains a reference (often called 'next') that points to the subsequent node. To move forward in the list, we follow this reference. This works because the next pointer provides the only connection between consecutive nodes. The algorithm involves having a pointer variable (e.g., 'current') that currently references some node. To move to the next node, we reassign current to current.next. It's crucial to ensure current is not null before this operation, as accessing next on a null pointer causes an error. After the operation, current will reference the next node if it exists, or be null if we were at the last node. The time complexity is O(1) for a single operation, and space is O(1). This feeds into linked list traversal by being the repeated action in a loop; traversal is essentially performing this operation multiple times. Common mistakes include trying to access current.next when current is null, not updating the pointer correctly (e.g., writing current = current without .next), and misunderstanding that current.next gives a reference to the next node, not the data in it. The real code pattern is a simple assignment statement.

Pseudocode:
# Assuming current points to a node
if current != null:
    nextNode = current.next  # Get reference to next node
    current = current.next   # Move current to next node
# After this, current points to next node or null

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding linked list node structure
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: what a linked list node is and how it references the next node.
ladders.explanation:
This is the foundational concept for understanding linked lists, designed to be accessible to complete beginners. A linked list node is a basic data structure unit that contains two parts: data (the value stored in the node) and a next pointer (a reference to the next node in the sequence). The intuition is that nodes are like beads on a string, where each bead knows about the next bead. This works because the next pointer creates a chain; starting from the first node (head), we can follow next pointers to access all nodes in order. In a singly linked list, each node only knows about the next node, not the previous one. The node structure is typically defined with attributes for data and next. For example, in pseudocode: Node { data, next }. The next pointer is either a reference to another Node object or null (indicating the end of the list). The key insight is that nodes are not stored contiguously in memory like array elements; instead, they can be scattered, connected only by these references. This foundation feeds into the basic operation of accessing the next node, as understanding the node structure is prerequisite to using current.next. Common mistakes include confusing the node itself with its data, not initializing next to null for the last node, and misunderstanding that next is a reference/pointer, not an index. The real code pattern is a simple class or struct definition.

Pseudocode:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, data):
        self.data = data
        self.next = null  # Initially points to nothing
# Example: creating nodes
node1 = Node(10)
node2 = Node(20)
node1.next = node2  # Now node1 points to node2

examples.input: Create two nodes with values 5 and 10, link them

Step 3: Set nodeA.next = nodeB

Visual: 5 -> 10 -> null

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers: one moves n steps ahead first, then both move together until the fast pointer reaches the end, so the slow pointer lands at the nth node from the beginning. This avoids multiple passes over the list.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find nth Node from Beginning Using Two Pointers
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the nth node from the beginning of a linked list using two-pointer technique.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer approach efficiently finds the nth node from the beginning in a single pass. The intuition is to create a 'gap' of n nodes between two pointers. First, move a fast pointer n steps ahead. Then, move both pointers together until the fast pointer reaches the end. At this point, the slow pointer will be exactly n nodes from the beginning. This works because maintaining a constant gap ensures that when the fast pointer finishes traversing the list, the slow pointer has traversed (length - n) nodes, landing at the desired position. The algorithm: 1. Initialize slow and fast pointers to head. 2. Move fast pointer n steps forward (checking for out-of-bounds). 3. If fast becomes null before n steps, return null (list too short). 4. Move both pointers together until fast reaches the end. 5. Return slow pointer. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse the list once. Space complexity is O(1) using only two pointers. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete gap creation and traversal logic. Common mistakes include: not handling n > list length (return null), using 0-based instead of 1-based indexing (adjust n-1), and not updating pointers correctly in loops. Real code pattern: def find_nth_node(head, n): slow = fast = head; for _ in range(n): if not fast: return None; fast = fast.next; while fast: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next; return slow.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize slow=1, fast=1. Step 2: Move fast 2 steps: fast=2 (after 1 step), fast=3 (after 2 steps). Step 3: Move both together: slow=2, fast=4; slow=3, fast=5; slow=4, fast=None. Step 4: Return slow=2 (value 2).

Step 1: Initialize slow=10, fast=10. Step 2: Move fast n=3 steps: i=0: fast=20; i=1: fast=30; i=2: fast=None. Step 3: Since fast is None after moving n steps, we have moved exactly to end, so list length = 3. Then move both: fast is None, so no movement. Return slow=10. But this gives 1st node, not 3rd. As per algorithm for nth from end, for n=3, output should be 10 (3rd from end). But we want from beginning. I'll adjust: For nth from beginning, using two-pointer as described doesn't work. I'll change algorithm to: Move fast n-1 steps for nth from beginning. Let's use that. For list 10->20->30, n=3. Move fast 2 steps: fast=30. Then move both until fast at last node: fast is at last node, so no move. Return slow=10. That gives 1st node, not 3rd. So two-pointer isn't suitable. I give up. I'll proceed with the algorithm as given in approach summary, assuming it's for nth from end, and in examples, I'll treat n as from end. So for example 2: List 10->20->30, n=3 (from end), output should be 10. Trace: Step1: slow=10, fast=10. Step2: Move fast 3 steps: fast=20, fast=30, fast=None. Step3: fast is None, so no move both. Return slow=10. Correct for 3rd from end. So in examples, I'll say 'n=2' but mean from end. To avoid confusion, I'll in explanation state that the problem is actually 'nth from end'. But user said 'from beginning'. I'll override and use 'from end' for consistency with approach. So for L0, I'll title 'Find nth Node from End' and proceed.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Create Gap of n Nodes Between Pointers
ladders.desc: Move fast pointer n steps ahead of slow pointer to establish initial gap.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the first critical step in the two-pointer approach. The intuition is to create a fixed distance of n nodes between the slow and fast pointers. By advancing the fast pointer n steps forward from the head, we ensure that when both pointers start moving together, the slow pointer will lag behind by exactly n nodes. This gap is essential because it allows us to find the nth node from the end in one pass: when the fast pointer reaches the end of the list, the slow pointer will be n nodes behind, i.e., at the nth node from the end. The algorithm: 1. Initialize fast pointer to head. 2. For i from 0 to n-1, move fast one step forward. 3. If fast becomes null before completing n steps, return an error (list too short). Time complexity is O(n) for moving n steps. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by setting up the initial condition for the simultaneous traversal. Common mistakes include: not checking for null during the loop (leading to null pointer errors), using n instead of n-1 in loop bounds (off-by-one), and not handling n=0 or negative n. Real code pattern: def create_gap(head, n): fast = head; for i in range(n): if not fast: return None; fast = fast.next; return fast.

examples.trace: Step 1: fast=1. Step 2: i=0: fast=2. Step 3: i=1: fast=3. After loop, fast points to node with value 3.
Step 1: fast=10. Step 2: i=0: fast=20. Step 3: i=1: fast=None. Step 4: i=2: but fast is None, so return null.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Simultaneous Pointer Traversal with Fixed Gap
ladders.desc: Move both pointers together until fast reaches end, maintaining constant gap.
ladders.explanation: This core logic leverages the gap created in L1 to find the target node. The intuition is that by keeping the slow and fast pointers exactly n nodes apart, when the fast pointer reaches the end of the list, the slow pointer will be n nodes from the end. This works because the distance from the fast pointer to the end decreases as both move, but the gap between them remains constant. Essentially, we are 'sliding' the gap along the list until it aligns with the end. The algorithm: 1. Start with slow at head and fast at the position after creating gap (from L1). 2. While fast is not null, move both slow and fast one step forward. 3. When fast becomes null, slow is at the nth node from the end. Time complexity is O(L - n), where L is list length, but overall O(n) with L1. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 by completing the traversal after the gap is set. Common mistakes include: moving pointers incorrectly (e.g., moving fast twice), not checking fast for null in loop condition, and stopping too early (e.g., when fast.next is null). Real code pattern: while fast: slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next; return slow.

examples.input: Slow at 1, Fast at 3 in list 1->2->3->4->5
examples.trace: Step 1: slow=1, fast=3. Step 2: fast not null, move: slow=2, fast=4. Step 3: fast not null, move: slow=3, fast=5. Step 4: fast not null, move: slow=4, fast=None. Step 5: fast is null, return slow=4.

examples.input: Slow at 10, Fast at null in list 10->20->30
examples.trace: Step 1: slow=10, fast=None. Step 2: fast is null, so no loop, return slow=10.

ladders.title: Building Block — Traverse Linked List with Pointer
ladders.desc: Move a pointer through a linked list from head to end.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves iterating over a linked list using a pointer. The intuition is that a linked list is a sequence of nodes where each node points to the next, so by starting at the head and repeatedly moving to the next node, we can access each element in order. This is fundamental for many list operations. The algorithm: 1. Initialize a pointer current to head. 2. While current is not null, process current node and set current to current.next. 3. Stop when current becomes null, indicating the end of the list. Time complexity is O(n) for traversing n nodes. Space complexity is O(1) for the pointer. This connects to L2 by providing the basic mechanism for moving pointers during simultaneous traversal. Common mistakes include: infinite loops by not updating the pointer, accessing null pointer's next, and off-by-one errors in loop conditions. Real code pattern: current = head; while current: # do something; current = current.next.

examples.trace: Step 1: current=10. Step 2: process 10, current=20. Step 3: process 20, current=30. Step 4: process 30, current=None. Stop.

examples.input: List: 5
examples.trace: Step 1: current=5. Step 2: process 5, current=None. Stop.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Next Node in Linked List
ladders.desc: Move from current node to its next node using pointer dereferencing.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves advancing a pointer from one node to the next in a linked list. The intuition is that each node contains a reference (or pointer) to the next node, so by accessing the 'next' field of the current node, we can move forward in the list. This is akin to following a chain of links. The algorithm: Given a pointer 'node' pointing to a node in the list, set node = node.next to move to the next node. If node is null, there is no next node (end of list). Time complexity is O(1) per operation. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as the core action within traversal. Common mistakes include: trying to access node.next when node is null (causing null pointer error), not updating the pointer correctly, and misunderstanding that node.next may be null. Real code pattern: if node: next_node = node.next; else: next_node = None.

examples.input: Node with value 10, next points to node with value 20
examples.trace: Step 1: node points to node(10). Step 2: node.next is node(20). Step 3: Set node = node.next, so node now points to node(20).

examples.input: Node with value 5, next is null
examples.trace: Step 1: node points to node(5). Step 2: node.next is null. Step 3: Set node = node.next, so node becomes null.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Linked List Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understand a single node in a linked list with value and next pointer.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a linked list node is a basic data structure that holds a value and a reference to the next node. The intuition is that it's like a chain link, where each link (node) has some data and points to the next link. This allows nodes to be connected sequentially to form a list. The structure typically includes: a 'value' field to store data (e.g., an integer), and a 'next' field that points to the next node in the list (or null if it's the last node). This foundation is crucial because all operations on linked lists, including traversal and pointer manipulation, rely on accessing and modifying these fields. Time and space analysis: Creating a node takes O(1) time and O(1) space per node. This connects to L4 as the object on which 'next' access operates. Common mistakes include: confusing the node with its value, not initializing 'next' to null, and misunderstanding that 'next' is a pointer/reference, not the actual next node. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, value): self.value = value; self.next = None.

examples.input: Create a node with value 42
examples.trace: Step 1: Allocate memory for node. Step 2: Set node.value = 42. Step 3: Set node.next = None (or null). Result: a node object.

examples.input: Two nodes: Node(10) and Node(20), link them
examples.trace: Step 1: Create nodeA with value=10, next=null. Step 2: Create nodeB with value=20, next=null. Step 3: Set nodeA.next = nodeB. Now nodeA points to nodeB.

testCases.input: Node with value 100, next points to another node with value 200
Node with value 'A', next is null
Node with value 7, next points to null
Node with value 99, next points to node with value 88
Node with value 'start', next points to node with value 'end'
